title: Possible Prophecies About Muhammad
subtitle: Islamic Claims of Prophecies in the Torah and Gospel, and Christian Scholarly Responses
summary: Islamic tradition identifies certain biblical passages as referring to the future coming of Muhammad. The most prominent examples are Deuteronomy 18:18 (the prophecy of a prophet like Moses) and John 14:26 (the Paraclete or Counselor). Christian scholarship generally interprets these differently, viewing them as fulfilled in other ways or misapplied to Muhammad. Both traditions engage in serious theological and linguistic argumentation over these texts.

seo.metaTitle: Islamic Prophecies About Muhammad in the Bible | Straight Path
seo.metaDescription: Explore Islamic claims about Deuteronomy 18:18 and John 14:26 as prophecies of Muhammad, Christian scholarly responses, and linguistic analysis of the Paraclete.

sections.title: Overview of the Debate
sections.body:
Within Islamic scholarship and apologetic tradition, certain passages in the Hebrew Bible and New Testament are interpreted as prophesying the coming of Prophet Muhammad. The most prominent interpretations involve Deuteronomy 18:18, which speaks of a prophet like Moses who will arise from among the brothers of the Israelites, and passages in the Gospel of John concerning the Paraclete (Greek: Paraklétos), understood by some in Islamic tradition as referring to Ahmad, identified with Muhammad.

Christian scholarship, representing the dominant historical interpretation of these texts within Christianity, generally rejects these identifications. Christian scholars typically argue that Deuteronomy 18:18 refers to the succession of prophets within Israel, or specifically to Joshua or to Jesus Christ. Regarding the Paraclete, Christian tradition unanimously interprets this term as referring to the Holy Spirit (Ruh al-Qudus) who would be sent after Jesus' ascension.

The debate hinges on questions of textual interpretation, linguistic analysis, and historical context. Both traditions bring serious hermeneutical and theological arguments to bear on these passages.

sections.title: Islamic Interpretation: Deuteronomy 18:18
sections.body:
Islamic tradition interprets Deuteronomy 18:18-19 as a direct prophecy of Prophet Muhammad. The reasoning rests on several key points.

First, the passage speaks of a prophet who will be raised up "from among your brothers" (min qirb ikhwatika). In Islamic interpretation, this phrase is understood to refer to the descendants of Ishmael, who is described in Genesis as Abraham's son. Muhammad, as a descendant of Ishmael, would thus fulfill this criterion. Christians, by contrast, typically understand "brothers" to mean fellow Israelites or other descendants of Jacob.

Second, the phrase "like you" (kamoka) is emphasized in Islamic exegesis. Muslims note that Deuteronomy 34:10-12 states that "there has not arisen a prophet in Israel like Moses," establishing that Moses held a unique place among Israelite prophets. Islamic scholars argue that if the prophecy in Deuteronomy 18:18 referred to another Israelite prophet, this statement in Deuteronomy 34:10 would be contradicted. Therefore, they conclude, the prophecy must refer to a non-Israelite prophet who would be comparable to Moses in his role and mission.

Third, Islamic sources emphasize that Muhammad, like Moses, received a law (Shari'ah), performed miracles, and led his people through trials similar to Moses' experience with the Israelites. This parallelism strengthens the Islamic argument that Muhammad fits the "prophet like Moses" description.

The Qur'an itself references this interpretation. Surah 7:157 describes believers who follow the Messenger and find him written in the Torah and Gospel that they possess. This verse is understood by Islamic scholars to support the view that prophecies about Muhammad exist in the Torah.

sections.title: Islamic Interpretation: The Paraclete as Ahmad
sections.body:
In Islamic tradition, certain passages in the Gospel of John concerning the Paraclete (Paraklétos in Greek, or al-Faraqlit/Parakletos in Arabic) are interpreted as references to Muhammad under a different name.

The Qur'an itself presents this interpretation explicitly. In Surah 61:6, it states that Jesus announced to the Children of Israel: "I am the messenger of Allah to you confirming what came before me of the Torah and bringing good tidings of a messenger to come after me, whose name is Ahmad." Islamic scholars identify Ahmad (from the root h-m-d, meaning "to praise") with Muhammad, suggesting that both names derive from the same Arabic root and convey the meaning of "the praised one."

Muslim interpreters then connect this Qur'anic account to the Gospel passages about the Paraclete. They argue that "Paraclete" in John 14:26, John 15:26, and John 16:7 originally referred to Ahmad/Muhammad, but was later misunderstood or mistranslated by Christian communities who came to interpret it as the Holy Spirit.

The Islamic argument for this identification rests on several linguistic and thematic points. First, they note that Ahmad and Muhammad are closely related names from the same root. Second, they point out that in John 16:8-11, the Paraclete is described as coming to "convict the world of sin, and of righteousness, and of judgment." Islamic scholars interpret this as a description of Muhammad's prophetic mission: to call people away from sin and toward righteousness through the revelation of the Qur'an. Third, they note that John 14:26 describes the Paraclete as coming to "teach you all things," which Muslims connect to Muhammad's role in transmitting divine guidance through the Qur'an and Sunnah.

sections.title: Christian Scholarly Response
sections.body:
Christian scholarship offers several substantive responses to Islamic interpretations of these prophecies.

Regarding Deuteronomy 18:18, Christian scholars present multiple interpretations. The most common view within mainstream Christian exegesis holds that this passage refers to the succession of prophets within Israel, or specifically to Joshua as the immediate successor of Moses. Some Christian scholars argue that the phrase "like you" does not mean perfectly identical but rather similar in function as a leader and lawgiver within the theocratic structure of ancient Israel. The statement in Deuteronomy 34:10 that "there has not arisen in Israel a prophet like Moses" is understood by Christian scholars as referring specifically to prophets within the covenant community of Israel, not as a universal statement excluding all non-Israelite prophets.

Additionally, Christian interpreters argue that the text's original historical context indicates it was intended to establish procedures for evaluating prophetic claims within ancient Israelite society, not to make a prophecy about a figure seven centuries in the future. The phrase "from among your brothers" would naturally be understood by the original Israelite audience as referring to future Israelite prophets, not to descendants of Ishmael.

Some Christian scholars also point to Jesus Christ as the ultimate fulfillment of Deuteronomy 18:18. They argue that Jesus came as the great prophet-teacher, the ultimate lawgiver through the New Covenant, and represents the culmination of the prophetic office in a way that surpasses all previous prophets.

Concerning the Paraclete passages, Christian scholarship is virtually unanimous that these refer to the Holy Spirit. Christian exegetes note that: (1) the context of John 14-16 clearly identifies the Paraclete as the Holy Spirit sent by the Father; (2) the term parakletos in Greek has a well-established meaning in early Christian literature referring to a helper, advocate, or comforter—not a human person; (3) the Paraclete is described as being sent "in" believers and "with" them, dwelling in them, language that applies to the Spirit rather than a human prophet; (4) Acts 2 records the Pentecost event as the fulfillment of Jesus' promise concerning the Paraclete; and (5) there is no historical evidence that early Christians understood these passages as referring to a future human prophet named Ahmad.

Christian scholars also note the chronological difficulty: if the Paraclete was meant to be Muhammad, who was born in 570 CE, the early Christians would have had to wait over 500 years for the fulfillment of Jesus' explicit promise. By contrast, the promise was fulfilled within weeks of Jesus' crucifixion, according to Christian tradition.

sections.title: Linguistic and Etymological Considerations
sections.body:
The debate over the Paraclete involves careful linguistic analysis, where scholars on both sides bring serious philological arguments.

The Islamic argument rests significantly on the proposed identity between "Ahmad" and "Paraclete." Islamic scholars argue that "Paraclete" (Paraklétos) in Greek could be a mistranslation or misunderstanding of an Aramaic or Hebrew original. They propose that the original text might have contained a word meaning "the praised one" (related to the Semitic root h-m-d), which was then rendered as "Paraklétos" through misunderstanding or deliberate alteration.

Some Islamic scholars have proposed that "Paraklétos" could derive from "Peri-Clutos" (meaning "famous" or "renowned"), which they argue could have been used to translate a Semitic term meaning "praised." They contend that this represents either a scribal error or a misunderstanding of the original meaning.

From the Islamic perspective, the absence of manuscript evidence supporting an alternative reading of the Paraclete passages is itself explained by the doctrine of tahrif—the belief that the Torah and Gospel were altered or corrupted by Jewish and Christian authorities over time. Islamic scholars argue that if the original biblical texts contained prophecies of Muhammad (whether under the name Ahmad, Periklytos, or another term), it would follow that later Christian copyists would have standardized the text to align with Christian interpretations of the Paraclete as the Holy Spirit, leaving no surviving manuscript variants to attest the original wording. From this perspective, the uniformity of manuscript attestation for Paraklétos as "Helper/Comforter" is itself evidence of successful textual alteration rather than evidence against the original prophecy.

Christian scholars counter these arguments on several grounds. First, they point out that Paraklétos is thoroughly attested in Greek literature predating Christianity, where it consistently means "advocate," "helper," or "comforter"—not "praised one." Lexical evidence shows the term was already in use in Greek with these meanings. Second, Christian scholars note that the Gospels were written in Koine Greek for Greek-speaking audiences, and there is no textual evidence of variant readings or manuscript traditions that support an alternative rendering. Third, they argue that if Muhammad was meant to be prophesied, it would be unusual for such a central prophecy to be obscured through mistranslation or wordplay rather than being clearly stated. Fourth, the historical context shows that the early Palestinian church used both Greek and Aramaic, yet we have no evidence from Aramaic texts, the Dead Sea Scrolls, or any early Jewish or Christian source that Paraklétos was ever understood as referring to a praised one or to Muhammad.

Linguistically, Christian scholars also point out that if "Ahmad" was the intended meaning, it would be strange for the Gospels to use a Greek term entirely unrelated in form or etymology to any Semitic word meaning "praised." The term "Ahmad" itself is not attested in any Palestinian Semitic sources from the 1st century CE, and its use as a name develops primarily in post-Islamic Arabia.

Both traditions agree that serious hermeneutical and linguistic work is required to interpret these passages fairly. The disagreement lies in the conclusions drawn from that analysis.

sections.title: Historical Development of These Interpretations
sections.body:
The Islamic interpretation of these biblical passages as prophecies of Muhammad developed within the first Islamic centuries and became increasingly sophisticated over time.

In the early Islamic period, as Muslim scholars encountered Christian communities and developed systematic Islamic theology, the identification of certain biblical passages with prophecies of Muhammad emerged as a significant element of Islamic apologetics. Early Muslim scholars such as al-Tabari (838-923 CE) and Ibn Kathir (1301-1373 CE) developed detailed exegetical arguments for these interpretations, drawing on Qur'anic references, hadith literature, and careful textual analysis.

The identification of the Paraclete with Muhammad became particularly prominent in later Islamic scholarship. By the medieval period, this interpretation appeared in numerous Islamic works of tafsir (Qur'anic exegesis) and apologetics. Muslim authors presented detailed linguistic arguments attempting to demonstrate how "Paraclete" could have originated from or been mistranslated from "Ahmad."

Christian responses to these interpretations also developed over time. In medieval Christendom, Christian scholars were generally aware of Islamic claims regarding Deuteronomy 18:18 and the Paraclete, as they encountered Muslim intellectual traditions through debate, scholarly exchange, and polemical literature. Christian responses typically emphasized textual fidelity, the internal consistency of Christian interpretation, and the historical testimony of early Christian sources.

In the modern scholarly context, both Christian and Islamic scholars have engaged in more rigorous historical-critical analysis. Modern Christian biblical scholars have developed increasingly sophisticated understanding of the original Greek, Aramaic, and Hebrew contexts. Similarly, modern Islamic scholars have refined their arguments with greater attention to linguistic evidence and manuscript traditions.

The contemporary scholarly debate benefits from improved access to primary sources, better understanding of the historical contexts of 1st-century Palestine and early Christian communities, and more sophisticated linguistic methodology. However, both traditions continue to maintain their traditional positions while engaging with the strongest versions of the other tradition's arguments.

sections.title: Scriptural Basis for the Islamic Perspective
sections.body:
The Qur'an provides the foundational Islamic perspective on these prophecies. Surah 7:157 explicitly states that believers recognize the Messenger written in the Torah and Gospel they possess. This verse affirms that Islamic tradition sees textual evidence for Muhammad's coming in the Torah and Gospel.

More directly, Surah 61:6 presents Jesus himself announcing the coming of Ahmad. In Islamic interpretation, this verse confirms that biblical prophecies regarding a messenger to come after Jesus were indeed intended references to Muhammad. The verse's inclusion in the Qur'an establishes, from the Islamic perspective, the validity of looking to the Torah and Gospel for such prophecies.

These Qur'anic references shape how Muslim scholars approach the biblical texts. When Muslim exegetes examine Deuteronomy 18:18 and the Paraclete passages, they do so already convinced by the Qur'an that prophecies of Muhammad exist in the earlier scriptures. This influences their interpretation methodology and strengthens their conviction that apparent Christian interpretations represent later misunderstanding or distortion of the original intent.

From an Islamic perspective, the presence of such prophecies in earlier scriptures provides evidence for the continuity of divine revelation and confirms Muhammad's status as the final messenger whose coming was foretold by the prophets who came before him.

sections.title: Christian Scriptural Context and Early Church Testimony
sections.body:
The Christian tradition grounds its interpretation of Deuteronomy 18:18 and the Paraclete passages in the broader narrative of the New Testament and early Christian testimony.

Regarding Deuteronomy 18:18, Christian interpreters note that the New Testament itself engages with the prophecy. Peter, in Acts 3:22-26, quotes Deuteronomy 18:15 and interprets it as referring to Jesus Christ: "Moses indeed said to the fathers, 'The Lord God will raise up a prophet for you from among your brothers, like me. You shall listen to him in all things whatever he says to you.'" This early Christian application of the passage to Jesus establishes the foundational Christian interpretation.

Concerning the Paraclete passages, the Gospel of John makes clear in its own narrative that Jesus is speaking of the Holy Spirit. John 14:26 explicitly states: "But the Counselor, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, he will teach you all things, and will remind you of all that I said to you." The phrase "the Holy Spirit" (to Pneuma to Hagion in Greek) directly identifies the Paraclete with the third person of the Christian Trinity.

Furthermore, the Acts of the Apostles records the fulfillment of this promise at Pentecost (Acts 2:1-4), where the Holy Spirit is poured out upon the disciples. This event, occurring approximately 50 days after Jesus' resurrection (and, per Acts 1:3, roughly ten days after his ascension), is understood by Christian tradition as the realization of Jesus' promise concerning the Paraclete.

Christian scholars emphasize that the early church, from its founding, understood and interpreted these passages in the manner described above. The consistency of this interpretation across early Christian literature, the various early church fathers, and different Christian traditions worldwide indicates that this was not a later corruption of meaning but the original understanding within the Christian community.

quranVerses.arabic: ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ ٱلرَّسُولَ ٱلنَّبِىَّ ٱلْأُمِّىَّ ٱلَّذِى يَجِدُونَهُۥ مَكْتُوبًا عِندَهُمْ فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَٱلْإِنجِيلِ يَأْمُرُهُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَىٰهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ ٱلطَّيِّبَٰتِ وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْخَبَٰٓئِثَ وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَٱلْأَغْلَٰلَ ٱلَّتِى كَانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَعَزَّرُوهُ وَنَصَرُوهُ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلنُّورَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ مَعَهُۥٓ ۙ أُو۟لَٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Those who follow the Messenger, the unlettered prophet, whom they find written in what they have of the Torah and the Gospel, who enjoins upon them what is right and forbids them what is wrong and makes lawful for them the good things and prohibits for them the evil and relieves them of their burden and the shackles which were upon them. So they who have believed in him, honored him, supported him and followed the light which was sent down with him - it is those who will be the successful.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَإِذْ قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ يَٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُم مُّصَدِّقًۭا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَمُبَشِّرًۢا بِرَسُولٍۢ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِى ٱسْمُهُۥٓ أَحْمَدُ ۖ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ قَالُوا۟ هَٰذَا سِحْرٌۭ مُّبِينٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: And [mention] when Jesus, the son of Mary, said, "O children of Israel, indeed I am the messenger of Allah to you confirming what came before me of the Torah and bringing good tidings of a messenger to come after me, whose name is Ahmad." But when he came to them with clear evidences, they said, "This is obvious magic."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: Yahweh your God will raise up to you a prophet from among you, of your brothers, like me. You shall listen to him. This is according to all that you desired of Yahweh your God in Horeb in the day of the assembly, saying, “Let me not hear again Yahweh my God’s voice, neither let me see this great fire any more, that I not die.” Yahweh said to me, “They have well said that which they have spoken. I will raise them up a prophet from among their brothers, like you. I will put my words in his mouth, and he shall speak to them all that I shall command him. It shall happen, that whoever will not listen to my words which he shall speak in my name, I will require it of him.
But the Counselor, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, he will teach you all things, and will remind you of all that I said to you.
“When the Counselor has come, whom I will send to you from the Father, the Spirit of truth, who proceeds from the Father, he will testify about me.
Nevertheless I tell you the truth: It is to your advantage that I go away, for if I don’t go away, the Counselor won’t come to you. But if I go, I will send him to you.
I will raise them up a prophet from among their brothers, like you. I will put my words in his mouth, and he shall speak to them all that I shall command him.

I will raise them up a prophet from among their brothers, like you. I will put my words in his mouth, and he shall speak to them all that I shall command him.
It shall happen, that whoever will not listen to my words which he shall speak in my name, I will require it of him.

Since then, there has not arisen a prophet in Israel like Moses, whom Yahweh knew face to face,
in all the signs and the wonders, which Yahweh sent him to do in the land of Egypt, to Pharaoh, and to all his servants, and to all his land,
and in all the mighty hand, and in all the awesome deeds, which Moses did in the sight of all Israel.
Since then, there has not arisen a prophet in Israel like Moses, whom Yahweh knew face to face,

For Moses indeed said to the fathers, ‘The Lord God will raise up a prophet for you from among your brothers, like me. You shall listen to him in all things whatever he says to you.
It will be that every soul that will not listen to that prophet will be utterly destroyed from among the people.’
Yes, and all the prophets from Samuel and those who followed after, as many as have spoken, they also told of these days.
You are the children of the prophets, and of the covenant which God made with our fathers, saying to Abraham, ‘In your offspring will all the families of the earth be blessed.’
God, having raised up his servant Jesus, sent him to you first to bless you, in turning away every one of you from your wickedness.”
Yahweh your God will raise up to you a prophet from among you, of your brothers, like me. You shall listen to him.

Now when the day of Pentecost had come, they were all with one accord in one place. Suddenly there came from the sky a sound like the rushing of a mighty wind, and it filled all the house where they were sitting. Tongues like fire appeared and were distributed to them, and one sat on each of them. They were all filled with the Holy Spirit, and began to speak with other languages, as the Spirit gave them the ability to speak.
To these he also showed himself alive after he suffered, by many proofs, appearing to them over a period of forty days, and speaking about God’s Kingdom.

faq.question: What is the Islamic argument that Deuteronomy 18:18 refers to Muhammad?
faq.answer: Islamic scholars argue that Deuteronomy 18:18's promise of a prophet "from among your brothers, like me" refers to Muhammad for several reasons: (1) The phrase "from among your brothers" is interpreted to mean from the descendants of Ishmael, Muhammad's lineage; (2) The statement in Deuteronomy 34:10 that no prophet like Moses arose in Israel is understood to mean a non-Israelite prophet is predicted; (3) Muhammad, like Moses, received law, performed miracles, and led his community. Christian scholars typically interpret "brothers" as meaning fellow Israelites and understand the prophecy as referring to Israelite prophets or to Joshua specifically.

faq.question: What does "Paraclete" mean, and why do Muslims connect it to Ahmad/Muhammad?
faq.answer: "Paraclete" (Greek: Paraklétos) appears in John 14:26, 15:26, and 16:7, and in Christian tradition means the Holy Spirit. Islamic scholars argue that "Paraclete" could be a mistranslation of an original Aramaic or Hebrew word meaning "the praised one," connecting it to "Ahmad" (Arabic: أحمد), which they interpret as derived from the root h-m-d meaning "praised." Christian scholars counter that Paraklétos is well-attested in Greek literature with the consistent meaning of "helper" or "comforter," and that early Christian sources unanimously understood it as the Holy Spirit.

faq.question: What does the Quran say about prophecies of Muhammad in the Torah and Gospel?
faq.answer: Quran 7:157 states that believers follow the Messenger whom they find written in the Torah and Gospel. Quran 61:6 presents Jesus saying: "bringing good tidings of a messenger to come after me, whose name is Ahmad." These verses form the Qur'anic basis for the Islamic interpretation that the Torah and Gospel contain prophecies of Muhammad, though they do not specify which passages these are.

faq.question: How do Christians interpret Deuteronomy 18:18?
faq.answer: Christian scholars offer several interpretations: (1) The most common view is that it refers to the succession of prophets within Israel or specifically to Joshua; (2) Some Christian interpreters apply it to Jesus Christ as the ultimate prophet and fulfillment of the law; (3) Christian exegetes emphasize the original context was meant to guide ancient Israelites in evaluating prophets within their own community. Christians note that the early church did not understand this as a prophecy of a figure centuries in the future.

faq.question: When did Islamic scholars first develop the interpretation that the Paraclete refers to Muhammad?
faq.answer: While the Qur'an itself (61:6) mentions Jesus predicting "Ahmad," the detailed scholarly identification of the Paraclete passages with Muhammad developed in the early Islamic centuries. Prominent medieval Islamic scholars like al-Tabari (9th-10th century) and Ibn Kathir (14th century) developed sophisticated exegetical arguments for this interpretation. The interpretation became increasingly refined over time as Muslim scholars engaged with Christian theology and developed detailed linguistic arguments.

faq.question: How do Christian scholars address the claim that the Paraclete should have come centuries later if it referred to Muhammad?
faq.answer: Christian scholars note that Jesus' promise of the Paraclete was fulfilled at Pentecost, approximately 50 days after his resurrection (roughly ten days after his ascension, per Acts 1:3), as recorded in Acts 2. If the Paraclete referred to Muhammad, Christians argue, it would be unprecedented for such a central promise to remain unfulfilled for over 500 years, contrary to the pattern of biblical prophecy fulfillment. Additionally, early Christian sources consistently testify to understanding the Paraclete as the Holy Spirit present from the Pentecost event onward.

furtherReading.title: Tafsir al-Tabari
furtherReading.author: Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari
furtherReading.note: Classical Islamic exegesis providing detailed commentary on Qur'an 7:157 and 61:6, including discussion of prophecies about Muhammad in earlier scriptures.

furtherReading.title: Tafsir Ibn Kathir
furtherReading.author: Ismail ibn Umar ibn Kathir
furtherReading.note: Medieval Islamic exegetical work containing comprehensive argumentation for identifying biblical prophecies as references to Muhammad.

furtherReading.title: The Gospel According to John, World English Bible
furtherReading.author: Anonymous; traditionally attributed to John
furtherReading.note: Primary Biblical text used for the Paraclete passages; the specific public-domain translation is identified for reproducibility.

furtherReading.title: Word Biblical Commentary: John
furtherReading.author: George R. Beasley-Murray
furtherReading.note: Modern Christian scholarly analysis of the Paraclete passages with detailed examination of Greek terminology and early Christian interpretation.
